does: This paper develops a decoding-time steering method to reduce hallucinations in medical vision-language models without modifying weights. The approach uses sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to identify feature directions in late model layers, then applies targeted interventions: boosting universal features that improve report quality and suppressing model-specific directions linked to clinical errors. Testing on three radiology VLMs (RadVLM, LLaVA-Rad, CheXOne) with the MIMIC-CXR dataset, the method achieves relative improvements of 5.4–17.0% on the clinical composite metric and significant gains across all models. The key finding is that quality-promoting directions transfer across architectures while hallucination-linked directions are model-specific, requiring architecture-dependent suppression lists rather than universal ones.
connects: This work extends recent advances in SAE-based model steering and mechanistic interpretability applied to language models, adapting those techniques to the medical vision-language domain where hallucination is a critical safety concern. It builds on prior work addressing hallucination in large models and leverages causal steering methods developed in interpretability research. The paper connects to earlier efforts in decoding-time intervention without weight updates, combining SAE analysis with clinical safety requirements specific to radiology. It responds to the documented problem of medical VLM errors in report generation and draws on literature examining cross-model transfer of interpretable features, extending those insights to the medical imaging setting where model-specific versus universal features have direct clinical implications.
enables: This work opens pathways for steering medical VLMs toward safer deployment without retraining, making mechanistic interpretability tools practical for high-stakes clinical applications. The finding that boost directions are universal while suppressions are model-specific suggests that future methods could develop shared libraries of safety-improving features while maintaining efficient, per-architecture suppression protocols. The approach could extend to other medical tasks (diagnosis, localization, severity assessment) and multimodal domains beyond radiology. It raises questions about systematic discovery of clinically relevant feature directions and how to scale steering to larger models and datasets. The method potentially enables real-time safety filtering during deployment and motivates deeper investigation into what makes certain directions transferable across medical model architectures.
